All Questions
Were You Asked This?
Design a prompt playground

Design a prompt playground platform that allows users to create, test, and iterate on AI prompts with different models and parameters.

Asked at:

OpenAI

A prompt playground is a web platform where people craft AI prompts, run them against different language models, tweak parameters like temperature or top_p, and compare outputs side-by-side. Think of it like Postman or GitHub Codespaces, but for LLM prompts—fast iteration, streaming results, history, and experiment runs.

Interviewers ask this to test how you design a latency-sensitive, experiment-heavy product with external dependencies (LLM providers), rate limits, cost tracking, and collaboration. It pushes you to balance interactive UX (streaming, low-latency) with batch work (evaluation suites), durable execution, reproducibility, and storage hygiene. Expect to reason about job orchestration, versioning, vendor abstraction, and real-time updates under realistic constraints.

Community Solutions
Design a prompt playground
by harsh varshney
3
13
Common Functional Requirements

Most candidates end up covering this set of core functionalities

Users should be able to create, edit, and version prompts with variables, choose models/providers, and configure parameters.

Users should be able to run prompts interactively with real-time streaming output and compare results across multiple models side-by-side.

Users should be able to define and execute batch experiments or test suites (multiple inputs x models x parameters) and view metrics like latency, cost, and pass/fail criteria.

Users should be able to save and share projects, and browse run history with diffs between prompt versions and outputs.

Common Deep Dives

Common follow-up questions interviewers like to ask for this question

How will you stream model outputs in real time while supporting side-by-side comparisons across providers?

How will you run large experiment suites (thousands of prompt-input combos) without violating provider rate limits or spiking latency for interactive users?

How will you guarantee reproducibility and trustworthy comparisons across runs and versions?

How will you keep storage and query costs under control as runs and outputs grow?

Relevant Patterns

Relevant patterns that you should know for this question

Real-time Updates

Interactive prompt testing relies on streaming tokens and live comparisons; users expect to see outputs evolve in real time and cancel mid-run. Real-time delivery (SSE/WebSockets) and session-scoped aggregation are essential to a responsive playground.

Read More
Managing Long Running Tasks

Batch experiments and evaluation suites are asynchronous, potentially large, and failure-prone due to provider limits. Durable jobs with retries, backoff, checkpoints, and idempotency keep the system reliable and cost-aware.

Read More
Multi-step Processes

Complex runs often include multiple steps—template rendering, input expansion, calling several models/tools, scoring, and summarizing results. A workflow approach (sagas/durable execution) coordinates these steps and handles partial failures cleanly.

Read More
Relevant Technologies

Relevant technologies that could be used to solve this question

API Gateway

An API Gateway centralizes authentication, request shaping, and routing for both interactive and batch lanes. It’s a natural choke point to enforce per-tenant rate limits, expose streaming endpoints, and provide unified observability for calls to multiple model providers.

Read More
Redis

Redis is well-suited for low-latency coordination: per-provider/tenant rate limiting (token buckets), ephemeral session state for streaming, lightweight queues (Streams) for background jobs, and caching model metadata. It helps keep interactive latency low and protect provider APIs.

Read More
PostgreSQL

PostgreSQL is a strong default for relational data like users, projects, prompt versions, run metadata, and experiment definitions. Its transactional guarantees help with reproducibility and comparisons, while JSONB columns can flexibly store structured run configs without sacrificing queryability.

Read More
Similar Problems to Practice

Related problems to practice for this question

Job Scheduler

Like a distributed scheduler, the playground must orchestrate many asynchronous jobs with retries, backoff, and checkpoints (large experiment suites), while isolating high-priority interactive tasks from bulk workloads.

Read
Practice
Google Docs

Collaborative editing and versioning of prompts echoes document editing: real-time updates, comments/sharing, and a clear history of changes that can be diffed and rolled back.

Read
Practice
Metrics Monitoring

The platform needs dashboards for latency, error rates, and cost per model/provider over time, mirroring monitoring systems that store high-cardinality metadata and precompute aggregates for fast reads.

Read
Practice
Red Flags to Avoid

Common mistakes that can sink candidates in an interview

Treating all runs as synchronous HTTP requests without streaming or cancellation

This leads to poor UX (no incremental output), timeouts under load, and runaway cost when clients disconnect. Interviewers expect SSE/WebSockets and explicit cancellation to control spend and latency.

Ignoring provider quotas and lack of backpressure for batch experiments

Bursting thousands of calls will trigger 429s, bans, and starve interactive traffic. You need per-tenant/provider rate limiting, worker concurrency controls, retries with backoff, and separate lanes for interactive vs batch.

Storing large outputs inline with metadata without retention or tiering

Mixing big blobs with hot metadata bloats indexes, slows queries, and inflates storage cost. You should separate metadata from payloads, apply TTL/archival policies, and precompute aggregates for dashboards.
